metadata.generator: Claude Sonnet 4.6 — direct synthesis from biological priors
metadata.data_source: Biological priors from Baca et al. 2013 Cell 153:666-677, Sheth et al. 2026 J.Phys.A 59:115601, and known prostate cancer genomics resources (TCGA/cBioPortal/COSMIC/ICGC/PCAWG literature). No live database was queried.
papers_used_as_context: Baca SC et al. 2013 Punctuated evolution of prostate cancer genomes. Cell 153:666-677
Sheth S, Arsuaga J, Sazdanovic R. 2026 Characterizing cancer chromosome aberration pathways using multigraphs. J.Phys.A Math.Theor. 59:115601
metadata.intended_use: AMG graph-based ML benchmark — candidate edge prediction, partial graph completion, graph scoring

generation_assumptions.breakpoint_position_basis: Known prostate cancer gene neighborhoods from TCGA, cBioPortal, COSMIC, ICGC/PCAWG, and published WGS literature. Positions are literature-informed approximations, not live DB outputs.
generation_assumptions.chromoplexy_model: Nonindependent rearrangement chains with deletion bridges per Baca 2013 ChainFinder model. ETS+ cases biased toward interchromosomal patterns near transcriptionally active loci. CHD1-deleted-like cases biased toward intrachromosomal, gene-poor regions.
generation_assumptions.amg_cycle_basis: Cycle structures (C_n notation) compatible with AMG exchange multigraph theory per Sheth 2026. Each case carries an implied cycle partition of n DSBs.
generation_assumptions.nepc_distinction: NEPC cases use AURKA/MYCN/RB1/TP53/SRRM4 gene neighborhood priors, favor intrachromosomal rearrangements, fewer interchromosomal chains, consistent with neuroendocrine transdifferentiation genomic patterns.
generation_assumptions.adenocarcinoma_distinction: Primary adenocarcinoma cases use ETS-fusion-associated patterns (TMPRSS2-ERG at 21q), AR signaling neighborhood, recurrent TSG loci (PTEN, NKX3-1, CDKN1B, RB1, TP53, CHD1, SPOP, FOXA1, SMAD4). Chromoplexy-style multi-chromosome chains predominate in complex cases.
generation_assumptions.held_out_rationale: 1-3 interactions per case are masked to create prediction benchmarks. Held-out interactions are selected from topologically informative positions (cycle-closing edges, chain-linking edges).

interactions.support_rationale: TMPRSS2-ERG intrachromosomal deletion fusion on chr21q22; canonical prostate cancer driver event linked to androgen-receptor-driven transcription
Intragenic deletion bridging PTEN exon boundaries; recurrent TSG inactivation in prostate adenocarcinoma
Weak adjacency signal suggesting possible deletion bridge linkage between ERG locus and PTEN; not conclusively chained

synthetic_plausibility_notes.implied_cycle_partition: 14 = 10+2+2 (C_10 main chromoplexy + two C_2 deletion bridges)

comparability_notes.spirit_comparison_to_baca_2013: The synthetic dataset mirrors the key chromoplexy properties described by Baca et al. 2013: (1) chained multi-chromosome rearrangements arising in nonindependent fashion, (2) involvement of known prostate cancer TSGs (PTEN, NKX3-1, CDKN1B, TP53, RB1, SMAD4, CHD1, SPOP, FOXA1), (3) TMPRSS2-ERG fusion as an anchor event in ETS+ cases, (4) deletion bridges linking breakpoints across fusions, (5) a range from simple 3-fusion chains to complex 40+ breakpoint events. Interchromosomal fraction in ETS+ adenocarcinoma cases (mean ~0.75) is calibrated to match the ETS+ tumor bias reported in Baca 2013 Figure 4B. NEPC cases follow a different pattern matching the sparse WGS NEPC data available in the cohort. The synthetic data intentionally avoids exact patient-specific breakpoint profiles or validated fusion sequences from the paper.
comparability_notes.spirit_comparison_to_sheth_2026: Each case carries an explicit AMG cycle structure label (C_n notation) consistent with the partition theory developed in Sheth et al. 2026. The held-out target interactions are placed at topologically informative positions corresponding to cycle-closing edges and chain-linking edges — exactly the missing edge problem analyzed in Sheth 2026 Section 7 and the motivation for probabilistic cycle completion. The cycle structure diversity (C_2 through C_12, with compound structures like C_8+C_2 and 2C_4) covers the range of partition complexities enumerated in Sheth 2026 Table 1 and Table 3 for up to 7 chromosomes and 6+ DSBs.
where_synthetic_data_is_reliable: Chromosome involvement rates calibrated to known prostate cancer WGS literature (chr17, chr21, chr10, chr8, chr13 are highest-frequency)
Distinction between adenocarcinoma (higher interchromosomal fraction) and NEPC (higher intrachromosomal fraction) is consistent with reported patterns
Gene neighborhood positions are accurate to within published reference genome coordinates for known prostate cancer loci
Cycle structure notation is mathematically consistent with AMG theory
Held-out target placement strategy is topologically motivated
Disease-type-specific gene prior sets (ETS/AR pathway vs MYCN/AURKA/SRRM4/RB1) are well-supported by literature
where_synthetic_data_is_less_reliable: Exact breakpoint positions within gene neighborhoods are approximations; real breakpoints cluster at specific exon boundaries not fully reproduced here
Confidence values are heuristically assigned and not calibrated to any real posterior distribution
Interaction type classification (fusion vs adjacency vs deletion_bridge_like) is simplified relative to real sequencing-derived evidence
The covariance structure between breakpoints within a single tumor is not modeled — real chromoplexy chains have strong topological dependencies that are absent in independent synthetic construction
NEPC cases are based on limited published WGS data; NEPC structural genomics is less characterized than adenocarcinoma
Secondary interaction signals (adjacency, weak chain support) have low calibration fidelity
comparability_notes.covariance_warning: CRITICAL WARNING: Synthetic data generated from independent biological priors cannot reproduce the full joint covariance structure of real prostate cancer genomes. Real chromoplexy chains have non-trivial topological dependencies: the selection of which chromosomes co-participate in a chain, which genes are co-disrupted, and which rearrangement types co-occur are all governed by nuclear architecture, replication timing, and AR/transcription-driven DNA damage that are not modeled here. Models trained on this synthetic data may underestimate inter-case covariance, overfit to the prior gene list, and may not generalize to uncommon rearrangement topologies. Direct comparison to held-out real WGS data is required before any downstream model relying on this synthetic benchmark can be trusted.

prior_based_assumptions: All breakpoint positions are based on published gene coordinates, not direct database queries or tumor-level WGS data
Chromoplexy chain topology is inferred from published descriptions of chain patterns, not from computational ChainFinder analysis of real data
NEPC structural patterns are extrapolated from limited published NEPC WGS cohorts (n<10 in most studies) and may not capture true NEPC genomic diversity
Interaction confidence values are heuristically assigned based on biological plausibility rather than statistical inference from real data
The choice of which gene loci are involved per case reflects literature priors and not the full spectrum of real rearrangement targets
Cycle structure assignments (C_n) are topology-consistent but not computationally derived from resolving ambiguous AMGs as in Sheth 2026
underdetermined_without_real_data: True inter-chain covariance structure across cases — which genes co-occur in chains within the same tumor
The distribution of chain lengths (number of rearrangements per chain) in real vs synthetic data
The frequency and position of deletion bridges relative to chain anchors
The ratio of closed cycles to open chains in real chromoplexy events
The precise positional clustering of breakpoints within gene neighborhoods (exon-level resolution)
Whether synthetic confidence calibration matches posterior probabilities from real ChainFinder or probabilistic cycle-completion models
The true false-negative rate for chain detection (how many real interactions are missed)
NEPC-specific breakpoint co-occurrence at MYCN/AURKA/RB1/TP53 loci and whether these form genuine chains vs independent events
synthetic_generation_bias: Over-representation of known prostate cancer genes — synthetic cases are biased toward the gene set listed in the biological priors, potentially underrepresenting novel or infrequent rearrangement partners
Symmetric chromosome selection bias — cases are constructed with diverse chromosomes but may not reflect the true skewed distribution of chromosome participation in real chromoplexy
Chain length distribution may be too uniform — real chromoplexy chains have a heavy-tailed distribution (many 3-5 fusion chains, few 40+ fusion chains) that is difficult to reproduce synthetically

Interaction type diversity may be insufficient — real WGS data distinguishes reciprocal translocations, non-reciprocal translocations, inversions, and deletion bridges with finer resolution than available here
NEPC cases may be too similar to each other in gene neighborhood composition given the limited NEPC prior gene set
Held-out target selection is non-random and topologically biased toward cycle-closing edges, which may make the benchmark easier than real missing-data problems
features_to_treat_cautiously: Confidence values for all interactions — these are heuristic and not calibrated to any real data distribution
Interchromosomal vs intrachromosomal fraction values — calibrated to general literature trends, not case-level tumor biology
Adjacency interaction type — weakest evidence class, position and confidence are most uncertain
closed_cycle_evidence field — assigned categorically without computational cycle resolution
Positions in gene-poor genomic contexts — CHD1-deleted-like cases involve non-coding breakpoints whose positions are particularly uncertain
NEPC cases — substantially less constrained by real data than adenocarcinoma cases
required_real_data_comparisons: Comparison of synthetic breakpoint position distributions to real prostate WGS breakpoint catalogs (e.g., PCAWG/ICGC prostate cohort, SU2C cohort) using KS-test or Wasserstein distance
Comparison of synthetic chain length distribution to real ChainFinder output from published prostate WGS cohorts
Comparison of synthetic chromosome involvement frequencies to real per-chromosome rearrangement rates from prostate WGS studies
Comparison of synthetic interchromosomal vs intrachromosomal fraction by disease subtype to real ETS+/ETS-/NEPC stratified data
Assessment of whether cycle structure assignments in synthetic data match the cycle structure inference outputs of AMG computational tools applied to matched-complexity real cases
Calibration of synthetic confidence values against a held-out real dataset using proper scoring rules (Brier score, log-loss)
Comparison of gene co-involvement frequencies in synthetic chains to real chromoplexy gene co-occurrence matrices from Baca 2013 supplementary tables
Evaluation of downstream ML model generalization error when trained on synthetic data vs real data (transfer learning evaluation)
